data.title: Aurora Cannabis: Downgrading On Regulatory Headwinds And Competitive Pressures - Hold
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Aurora Cannabis Inc. or "Aurora Cannabis" (NASDAQ:ACB, TSX:ACB:CA) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier article on the company.

Q1 FY2026 Earnings Review

Earlier this week, Aurora Cannabis reported mixed Q1/FY2026 results, with higher-than-expected revenues offset by lower profitability. Otherwise noted, all numbers are in Canadian dollars. Please note also that comparisons are made on a quarter-over-quarter basis.

While both medical and consumer cannabis sales declined sequentially, seasonally higher contributions from the Bevo Farms' plant propagation business lifted overall revenues to a new all-time high.

However, quality issues and unsold crop inventory negatively impacted plant propagation margins during the quarter. As a result, the company's consolidated gross margin decreased to a new twelve-month low.

Adding insult to injury, Bevo Farms violated debt covenants, as outlined in more detail in a regulatory filing:

As at June 30, 2025, Bevo was not in compliance with its fixed charge coverage ratio financial covenant. Accordingly, the Term Facilities are classified as current in the interim condensed consolidated statements of financial position. In addition, subsequent to June 30, 2025, Bevo was not in compliance with a non-financial covenant to provide audited financial statements. Bevo is in discussions with the lender to obtain a waiver for the breaches and enter into an amended credit agreement.

After being poked on the issue by an analyst on the conference call, management expressed confidence in the subsidiary's ability to amend the terms of its secured credit facility in the near future.

While medical cannabis sales in Europe were impacted by previously-communicated regulatory changes in Poland, the almost 20% sequential decline in Australia was a negative surprise. As management did not outline specific issues, I would attribute the weak performance to competitive pressures.

Adjusted EBITDA of $10.8 million was down for a second consecutive quarter. However, free cash flow of $9.2 million was up sequentially.

Aurora Cannabis ended the fiscal year with $140.2 million in cash and cash equivalents and $45.8 million in restricted cash, as well as $59.8 million in non-recourse debt related to the Bevo Farms plant propagation business.

The company's core cannabis operations continue to be debt-free.

Outlook

On the conference call, management warned of potential disruptions in Germany as the new government prepares to impose restrictions on telehealth prescriptions.

However, no impact is expected in the current quarter, thus resulting in projected growth of 8% to 12% in the company's core medical cannabis business.

With lower-margin plant propagation sales expected to decline materially from Q1 peak season levels, consolidated gross margin should recover quite meaningfully.

In the earnings press release, management guided for "strong positive adjusted EBITDA" but warned of "significant cash outflows, in line with historical trends."

Valuation & Price Target

Given the combination of competitive pressures and upcoming regulatory changes in the company's largest market, I have lowered my profitability expectations even further.

With material growth in medical cannabis sales unlikely in the near-term, I have also reduced my assigned EV/Adjusted EBITDA multiple:

My price target is now based on a multiple of 6x estimated FY2026 adjusted EBITDA, which is roughly in line with U.S. multi-state operators and slightly below the average of Canadian licensed producers.

As a result, I am lowering my price target from USD10.00 to USD5.65 and downgrading my rating from "Buy" to "Hold."

Bottom Line

Aurora Cannabis reported mixed first quarter results, with medical cannabis sales pressured by regulatory changes and competition. In addition, the company's plant propagation subsidiary experienced quality issues and violated debt covenants.

While management expects the core medical cannabis segment to return to sequential growth in the current quarter, the company warned of upcoming regulatory changes in Germany, Aurora Cannabis' largest market, which could significantly impact sales in the second half of FY2026 and beyond.

Given persistent headwinds, I am downgrading the company's shares from "Buy" to "Hold."

In hindsight, I have underestimated the potential impact of regulatory changes and increased competition. Apologies for a bad call.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Navitas Semiconductor: A High-Stakes Gamble On The Future Of AI Data Centers
data.description:
Despite a promising long-term opportunity in next-gen data centers, Navitas Semiconductor (NASDAQ:NVTS) has seen its shares drop 16% after reporting Q2 earnings, highlighted by weak Q3 guidance. The weak guidance is a direct result of the company entering a transitional period where it is reducing its exposure to the smartphone charger market to focus on a potentially generational opportunity in Nvidia’s (NVDA) 800V data centers.

In my opinion, Navitas’ new strategy could achieve significant returns for both the company and its shareholders, thanks to the multi-billion-dollar market it would be exposed to, as well as its better profitability profile compared to the company’s existing markets. As is, the company estimates Nvidia’s next-gen architecture to create an annual $2.6 billion opportunity for power semiconductors by 2030.

With that in mind, AI data centers can prove to be a $600 million revenue opportunity for Navitas in 2030, thanks to its advanced products that are a perfect fit for Nvidia’s next-gen data center architecture. This could be possible due to Navitas’ deep expertise in GaN, being the first company to commercialize GaN, its more than 300 patents, and being one of 2 companies in Nvidia’s ecosystem capable of offering high and medium voltage GaN and ultrahigh voltage SiC.

Despite this, Navitas’ strategic business transition exposes the company to heightened concentration risk, meaning that it has to execute flawlessly through the end of FY 2026 to realize the benefits of this market opportunity. As such, I’m downgrading Navitas from a strong buy to a moderate buy at current levels.

Q2 Overview

In Q2 2025, Navitas reported a 29% YoY revenue decline from $20.5 million to $14.5 million as it navigates a challenging semiconductor industry that is currently in the midst of a cyclical slowdown. Furthermore, the company’s GAAP gross margin, including amortization, turned negative this quarter at -11.8% compared to 19.7% in the prior year. On a non-GAAP basis, however, the company reported a gross margin of 38.5%, down from 40.3% in the prior year.

The reason for the margin decline is Navitas reporting a $3.2 million SiC inventory reserve due to soft demand in China, as a direct result of the ongoing tariff environment, since Navitas’ SiC products are manufactured in the US, making them subject to tariffs by the Chinese government.

In terms of costs, Navitas reported a 31% YoY improvement in GAAP operating loss from $30.3 million to $21 million, with R&D costs declining 39.4% YoY and SG&A declining 49.6% YoY.

Based on its lower costs, Navitas reported a 33% YoY improvement in cash burn from $17.9 million to $11.9 million. At the end of Q2, Navitas held $161 million after raising $100 million through two prior equity offerings and zero debt on its balance sheet.

For Q3, Navitas is guiding for $10 million in revenues, representing a 31% sequential decline, a non-GAAP gross margin of 38.5%, in line with Q2 results, and non-GAAP operating costs of $15.5 million, implying a non-GAAP operating loss of $11.65 million.

Business Transition

Navitas’ weak revenue guidance and low operating cost levels are a direct result of its plan to reduce its exposure to mainstream mobile chargers, 65- and 45-watt, that are characterized by low margin profiles and their high exposure to China. Instead, the company is shifting all of its focus to the AI data center market to capitalize on the opportunity brought by its collaboration with Nvidia for the next-gen 800V HVDC data center architecture.

Meanwhile, the company is becoming more selective in the mobile sector by mainly focusing on ultrafast chargers with speeds higher than 100 watts. This can be seen by its recent collaboration with Xiaomi for a 90-watt GaN charger that has the size of a typical 12-watt silicon charger, showcasing the advantages of its GaN technology.

Accordingly, Navitas expects to lose a significant portion of its revenues in the next 2 quarters; however, its gross margin guidance highlights the profitability of this decision as it expects a similar gross margin profile in Q3 despite an expected 31% revenue decline. This highlights the more favorable gross margins of ultrafast chargers compared to mainstream ones, validating the company’s decision, in my opinion.

At the same time, the company intends to minimize its investments as it expects to maintain a quarterly cash burn level of $10–11 million in the near term to preserve its cash balance. Considering that Navitas exited Q2 with $161 million in cash, the company is likely to exit FY 2025 with around $138-140 million in cash at its current expected cash burn rate. This provides the company with ample runway to execute on its strategy, as it intends to ramp up its R&D investments later to support its opportunity in AI data centers.

With that in mind, Navitas’ expected revenue slowdown from this strategy will be potentially offset in FY 2026 as it starts tapping into its $450 million design wins, highlighted by 75 customer designs in current-gen data centers. This expected ramp in data center revenues could help boost Navitas’ growth rates next year and increase its gross margins due to the segment’s higher margin profile, as stated by management in the Q2 earnings call.

As is, these revenues will be significantly less impacted by Chinese tariffs since its AI data center customers are heavily based in the US. This could directly reduce the company’s cash burn rate in FY 2026 as it prepares for a major ramp in 2027 to earn its first 800V data center revenues.

On that note, Navitas’ data center ramp starting in FY 2026 coincides with the timeline of migrating its GaN manufacturing from TSMC to Powerchip, which was announced in June. This manufacturing shift is to capitalize on Powerchip’s capabilities since it utilizes 8-inch wafers, whereas TSMC utilizes 6-inch wafers. In this way, Navitas can get 80% more chips per wafer, which leads to lower production costs, more attractive prices for customers, and higher gross margins for Navitas.

This migration will occur throughout the next 12 to 24 months, with qualification of initial devices expected in Q4, and the migration of the company’s 100V GaN family to take place in the first half of 2026. In this way, Navitas could see its gross margins improve slightly in FY 2026, considering that this product family mainly targets chargers for smartphones.

Data Center Opportunity

In addition to the transitional stage plan, Navitas shared more details regarding its market opportunity in AI data centers in its Q2 earnings for the first time, mainly revolving around Nvidia’s 800 VDC architecture. This company forecasts this opportunity to be $3 billion annually by 2030, of which $2.6 billion will be in 800V data centers.

According to the company’s estimates, the first stage of this architecture, converting AC grid power to 800V DC, requires around $10 million of power semiconductors per GW of power delivered. Navitas also estimates that the second stage of the architecture, converting 800V DC to 48V DC, requires $15 million of power semiconductors per GW of power delivered.

As for the third stage, converting 48V DC to 12V DC to power the AI GPU, Navitas estimates it to represent the largest opportunity, requiring $20 million in power semiconductors per GW of power delivered. This brings the total power semiconductor content in the architecture to $45 per kW of power delivered.

At the same time, Navitas forecasts total data center power to reach 71 GW in 2030, of which 80% will be in 800V data centers. Accordingly, the first stage in Nvidia’s architecture presents a $570 million opportunity, the second stage presents an $855 million opportunity, and the third stage presents a $1.14 billion opportunity.

AI Data Centers

Total Power (GW)

71

Stage 1

$710,000,000

Stage 2

$1,065,000,000

Stage 3

$1,420,000,000

Total TAM

$3,195,000,000

800 V Data Centers

Total Power (GW)

57

Stage 1

$570,000,000

Stage 2

$855,000,000

Stage 3

$1,140,000,000

TAM

$2,565,000,000

Stage 1

As mentioned in my last coverage, Nvidia’s next-gen architecture will see an increase in semiconductor content due to the usage of SSTs to convert 13.8kV AC grid power down to 800V DC. This approach requires ultrahigh-voltage SiC, 3.3kV at least, due to SiC’s higher breakdown voltage than traditional silicon, lower switching and conduction losses, and its ability to operate at higher temperatures. All of these characteristics make ultra-high-voltage SiC power semiconductors crucial to this architecture since they emphasize performance and cost efficiencies.

This stage is where Navitas will shine, in my opinion, since it is currently the only company capable of offering SiC voltage ranges up to 6.5kV and is already working to reach 10kV. The company’s closest competitors in this stage are likely to be Infineon (OTCQX:IFNNY) and OnSemi (ON), which Nvidia recently added to the architecture’s ecosystem along with Innoscience, Analog Devices (ADI), and Renesas (OTCPK:RNECY), as both companies offer SiC voltage ranges up to 3.3kV.

On that note, Navitas’ management shared in the Q2 earnings call that the company has completed initial sample evaluations with customers, is receiving positive feedback, and is developing engineering samples that will begin shipping in Q4. In this segment, the company is targeting Schneider, Eaton, and Vertiv as potential customers and expects potential customers to create system designs in 2026 ahead of a volume production ramp in 2027.

Given the stringent ultrahigh voltage requirements for this stage and Navitas’ significant leadership in ultrahigh voltage SiC, I believe it can secure an 80% share in this stage. This could see the company realize $456 million in annual revenues from the first stage in 2023.

Stage 2

The second stage of the architecture requires 800V to 48V DC/DC converters that are directly integrated onto the server motherboard, meaning that it requires high frequency, efficiency, and density for thermal management. The 800V input of this stage requires high-voltage GaN or high-voltage SiC to convert the power down to 48V.

The decision to implement GaN or SiC in the input of this stage will depend on hyperscalers’ preferences. For hyperscalers prioritizing switching speeds, GaN would be the preferred choice, while SiC would be preferred by hyperscalers prioritizing power handling and thermal robustness, thanks to SiC’s ability to handle higher currents at high voltages.

As for the output of this stage, medium-voltage GaN is required due to its ability to handle high currents at lower voltages. Currently, Navitas is developing final engineering samples for lead customers for shipping in Q4, as is the case with the first-stage products. The company expects early production ramps in late 2026, with high-volume production expected in 2027.

The main players in this stage, in my opinion, are Navitas and Infineon, since both companies are capable of offering the high-voltage GaN and SiC, and the medium-voltage GaN required in this stage. This gives both Infineon and Navitas a strategic edge in this stage in terms of providing a full solution to customers, which could see both companies gain a higher content share compared to the other players in the architecture ecosystem.

In light of this, I believe Navitas can secure a 12% share in this stage, which translates to potential annual revenues of $102.6 million in 2030.

Stage 3

The third and final stage of the architecture requires 48V DC/DC converters to convert the 48V from the second stage down to 12V to power the AI processor. As this converter will be the closest to the AI processor on the motherboard, it demands high efficiency, density, and reliability in order not to damage the processor.

This stage will be the largest one in the architecture since it is the point of load conversion stage, where the whole architecture branches out. It involves converting 48V DC to lower voltages required by the GPU, memory, and other chips on the server board. Therefore, this stage will have a significant number of components, making it the largest in terms of total chip volume.

As is the case with the output of the second stage, medium-voltage GaN will be used in this stage, given these demands. To that end, Navitas intends to deliver final samples in Q4 and expects early production ramps in late 2026.

Despite being the largest stage, the third stage of the architecture is where hyperscalers will likely place higher emphasis on cost. Unlike the earlier 2 stages requiring niche ultra-high voltage SiC and high-voltage GaN, the medium-voltage GaN required in this stage is widely available through all of the suppliers within the ecosystem.

Given this intense competition and the high volume of power semiconductors required, hyperscalers may attempt to drive down costs by acquiring better cost-per-watt power semiconductors. While Navitas’ manufacturing shift to Powerchip can help in this regard, it still competes with major players in GaN power semiconductors. These players can reduce their prices significantly due to their high-volume manufacturing capabilities, which could undercut Navitas’ pricing power.

Based on this, I believe Navitas could secure a 5% share in this stage, which would translate to $57 million in revenues from this stage in 2030. Overall, the 800V data center environment could see Navitas generate $615.6 million in annual revenues in 2030.

Risks

While the long-term opportunity presented by the 800V data center architecture justifies Navitas’ business transition, it raises a significant concentration risk as the company appears to be putting all its eggs in one basket. Accordingly, Navitas has to execute flawlessly on its transition in order to realize this opportunity.

This means that Navitas has to maintain its technological edge in SiC and GaN power semiconductors, and customer sampling has to be favorable to receive customer orders for the 800V data center architecture. As is, the company is competing with industry heavyweights for content within this next-gen architecture, which means that if any of them develop or introduce superior products, its opportunity in 800V data centers could be severely impacted.

Conclusion

In summary, I remain bullish on Navitas after its Q2 earnings. While the company’s near-term business prospects may not be ideal, its transition to shed its exposure to mainstream chargers to focus on the 800V data center architecture could prove to be a profitable decision in the long term.

With that in mind, the expected decline in the company’s business this year could be offset by the expected ramp in current-gen data centers in FY 2026 when it starts tapping into its 75 customer designs in that segment. Furthermore, the company could see a boost in its gross margin next year as it completes the transition of its 100V GaN product family to Powerchip, which allows for the production of 80% more chips per wafer.

As for the 800V data center opportunity, I expect Navitas to generate $615.6 million in annual revenue from this segment in 2030. The main driver for this opportunity, in my opinion, is Navitas’ industry-leading SiC capabilities, offering up to 6.5kV and working to reach 10kV.

This could help the company dominate the first stage of Nvidia’s next-gen architecture. Meanwhile, the company’s high and medium-voltage GaN, combined with its SiC capabilities, positions it alongside Infineon to gain a higher share in the second stage relative to the other ecosystem members.

Despite this significant market opportunity, Navitas’ decision to exit mainstream chargers to place a higher focus on this opportunity exposes it to significant concentration risk, as the company’s long-term prospects are now tied to its success in 800V data centers. Given this risk, I’m maintaining my bullish outlook on Navitas but downgrading it from a strong buy to a moderate buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Putting Morgan Stanley Direct Lending's 11%+ Dividend Payout Under The Microscope
data.description:
Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (NYSE:MSDL) is one of the most conservatively postured business development companies (BIZD) in the public markets today, with a particularly close resemblance to Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) given its near pure-play focus on first lien senior secured loans and relatively low management fees for being externally managed. Additionally, both are backed by highly regarded external managers—BXSL by Blackstone (BX) and MSDL by Morgan Stanley (MS). In this article, I am going to take a fresh look at MSDL after its most recent earnings release, with a particular focus on whether or not its attractive dividend—currently yielding over 11%—is sustainable moving forward.

The Bull Case For MSDL's Stock And Dividend

There is certainly a lot to like about MSDL right now. In addition to the aforementioned strengths, it is quite discounted relative to its quality, as it currently trades at a whopping 13.6% discount to its net asset value at a time when peers like BXSL trade at a premium to NAV. This is pretty substantial given that, over the long term, it has not only sustained but actually grown its book value per share, proving itself to be a quality underwriter and allocator of capital.

Additionally, its underwriting performance continues to be excellent, with only 1.7% of the portfolio in its riskiest ratings of 3 and 4, and 98.3% of the portfolio in internal risk ratings 1 or 2. In particular, underwriting quality improved sequentially, with the bottom two ratings falling from 1.8% to 1.7% while the top two ratings increased. Internal risk rating 1 improved from 1.1% to 2.3%. Non-accruals were only 0.7% of the portfolio at cost-below blue-chip peers like Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC) and well below the broader industry average.

Furthermore, PIK income declined sequentially and now stands at just 3.9% of the overall portfolio's total investment income, giving it one of the lowest PIK rates in the BDC sector. As already mentioned, it also has outsized exposure to senior secured first-lien loans, with 96% of the portfolio in that category and another 2% in second-lien senior secured loans, leaving just 2% of the portfolio in equity and other types of investments. Over the past five quarters, virtually all new investments have been into first-lien and senior secured loans.

The portfolio is well diversified across 34 industries with 214 total borrowers, limiting individual position risk. Its top 10 investments make up just 15% of the total portfolio, with the largest investment accounting for just 1.9%. Additionally, 95% of its industry exposure is in non-cyclical sectors, which positions it well to weather potential economic headwinds. Management also emphasized that it is relatively well-sheltered from potential tariff impacts.

Another reason to like MSDL right now is its strong balance sheet, with an investment-grade credit rating and no debt maturing until 2027. Moreover, the vast majority of its debt does not mature until 2029 and 2030, and it recently paid off its $275 million unsecured note maturing in September of this year that carried a 7.55% interest rate. However, that note was not paid off until June, at the very end of Q2, so the full benefit will not be seen until the next quarter. The company also issued low-cost financing at an attractive rate, improving liquidity and its funding profile.

On top of all those strengths, this past quarter, its net debt-to-equity ratio increased from 1.07x to 1.10x. Management sees the optimal range as 1.15x to 1.20x, so they have further leverage capacity to enhance net investment income per share. Moreover, that leverage increase was heavily back-weighted in the quarter, meaning even the recent increase will have a larger impact in Q3 than it did in Q2.

Finally, MSDL is seeing signs of bottoming in spread compression across the sector, which could ease what has been a recent headwind-tightening spreads and lower going-in yields relative to the cost of debt. Greater stability in spreads should support net investment income per share.

When you combine all these tailwinds, the $0.50 net investment income per share generated in the recent quarter should move higher on a rate-neutral basis. Yes, the Fed may cut interest rates soon (a topic discussed later in this article), but at a bare minimum, management signaled they are very confident in the $0.50 per share dividend for the foreseeable future. This is especially true because they have $0.82 per share of spillover income, well above their $0.50 per share target, giving them additional liquidity to support the base dividend even if net investment income dips below $0.50 for a few quarters.

The Bear Case For MSDL's Stock and Dividend

That said, there are several reasons to be increasingly concerned about the durability of MSDL's dividend. The biggest reason is that coverage is exactly at 1.0x right now, which puts significant pressure on MSDL's management to come up with ways to create additional margin of safety for the dividend. While we have already discussed several of these, the fact remains that if MSDL were to experience an uptick in non-accruals, many of these initiatives would be offset, thereby keeping the pressure on the dividend.

In addition, if the Federal Reserve follows through with aggressive rate cuts over the next year—as the market is currently pricing in—MSDL, which invests in 100% floating-rate loans, will likely see its net investment income decline materially, even though it does have a good portion of its liabilities in floating-rate debt as well.

Aside from these two risks, there is also the fact that spreads have been tightening in the sector, as a lot of capital has been pouring in, putting lenders in an increasingly disadvantageous position. While management emphasized on the earnings call that they believe this has reached a trough, there is no guarantee that this is indeed the case. Only time will tell.

It is also worth noting that MSDL has added quite a few new investments since its IPO just over a year ago, which means that many of these loans have not yet experienced a true test of their credit quality. This could mean that some underwriting issues might pop up in the next year or two. Of course, once again, only time will tell, and we do have considerable confidence in MSDL's underwriting and workout capabilities.

Investor Takeaway

Overall, MSDL remains a very attractive investment opportunity in our view, with a more than 11% dividend yield and a double-digit discount to net asset value—even at a time when many of its similar peers, like BXSL and ARCC, trade at premiums to net asset value. Given that the underwriting quality remains strong and there is a viable path to sustaining the dividend for the next several quarters at least—due to the spillback income, the improved funding profile that has yet to carry through to the bottom line, and the additional leverage capacity on the balance sheet—we think that MSDL is a Strong Buy.

However, three to four quarters out, the company may have to take a much sharper look at its dividend sustainability. Given that it is likely the Fed will have cut rates materially by then, there is always a chance that a recession may be coming due to tariff impacts, and the company may have to be digging into its spillback income already at that point if one or two of the other aforementioned headwinds indeed manifest themselves in the coming quarters.

Notwithstanding, MSDL remains one of our higher-conviction BDCs at the moment, and we remain long alongside a few other high-conviction BDC picks at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Global Business Travel Group Q2: CWT Acquisition Set To Accelerate Margin Expansion
data.description:
Shares of Global Business Travel Group (GBTG) reacted positively to Q2 results, where guidance for both revenue growth and margins was raised. Furthermore, its acquisition of CWT was cleared by the FTC and is on track to be completed this quarter, potentially boosting GBTG's future FCF generation once synergies have been realized. While revenue growth expectations remain muted, I stick to the view I expressed in my previous writeup:

This is a margin expansion story, driven by the operating leverage in the business, which should see earnings and FCF growth comfortably exceeding revenue growth in the coming years.

With shares trading at 13 times my estimates for next year's FCF post the CWT acquisition, despite my outlook for solid FCF growth in the medium term, I am inclined to maintain a neutral stance given the execution risks I see as well as the elevated level of stock-based compensation ("SBC") expenses. Therefore, GBTG stock remains a hold for me.

Q2 Margins Shine Amid Tepid Revenue Growth

GBTG posted Q2 revenue growth of just 1% year over year, with full-year revenue guidance calling for 3% growth at the midpoint. Despite its leading market position, these growth rates pale in comparison to the global business travel market, which is estimated to be growing at 6.6% this year and forecasted to grow at 8.1% in 2026.

However in GBTG's case, its highlight is its impressive margins and the operating leverage in the business model, where incremental revenue generated on its platform drives limited additional expense. As shown below, cost of revenue and G&A expenses declined year over year, allowing management to spend more on growth initiatives related to technology and sales and marketing. Overall, adjusted operating expenses were down 2% year over year on a constant currency basis, with management highlighting that traveler care cost per transaction improved by 5% year over year due to higher efficiency and improved productivity coming from technology investments.

While the midpoint for full-year revenue guidance was lifted from no growth expected earlier to now 3% growth expected, adjusted EBITDA growth was raised to 9% compared to 7% expected previously. It is important to note that half of the revenue growth was attributed to favorable currency movements, which however does not impact adjusted EBITDA. The $523 million in adjusted EBITDA expected for this year implies a margin of 21%, expanding by 130 basis points compared to FY 2024. The midpoint of FCF guidance was also raised by $10 million to $150 million. Adjusting for certain non-recurrent expenses, the underlying FCF for FY 2025 is expected to be approximately $200 million, without accounting for any contribution from CWT.

CWT Acquisition: Quantifying the Financial Impact

While GBTG's management provided no guidance with respect to the contribution expected this year from CWT, I will turn to the investor presentation that was released when this deal was initially announced, to come up with my estimates. While these figures are not up to date, they appear to be still valid. According to the recently revised agreement, GBTG will issue 50 million shares together with $70 million in cash to the sellers of CWT.

CWT's adjusted EBITDA in FY 2024 was $71 million, and as shown above, GBTG's management is aiming to boost its contribution by finding synergies totaling $155 million over a 3-year period. Assuming CWT's performance has continued to remain flat compared to FY 2024, I expect its annual FCF to be around $50 million on a stand-alone basis. If we also assume that GBTG's management executes only a third of the targeted level of synergies in FY 2026, I would expect the FCF contribution from CWT to be at least $75 million.

Meanwhile, I expect GBTG, excluding CWT, to be able to grow FCF by at least 10% to $220 million next year, assuming no changes in working capital. On a combined basis, this translates to $295 million in FCF for next year. According to me, 10% FCF growth for GBTG excluding CWT is quite conservative, as lower interest expenses alone should help lift FCF as the level of debt gradually declines in the coming quarters. Notably, interest expenses are currently at an annual run rate of $90 million.

Following the CWT acquisition, which involves a cash payment of $70 million and taking on debt of $95 million, GBTG is expected to have a net debt of approximately $1 billion, based on where its balance sheet stood at the end of Q2. With net debt amounting to just over three times next year's FCF, the company remains in a solid financial position to continue to de-lever the balance sheet through FCF generation. As the debt level declines over the medium term, interest expenses will also be lower, which, together with the post-2026 synergies, should help lift FCF higher.

GBTG Stock Valuation

Assuming the CWT acquisition is closed, the total number of GBTG shares outstanding will increase by 50 million to 529 million. At the current share price, this translates to a market cap of $3.84 billion, implying a Price to FCF multiple of 13 based on my FY 2026 FCF estimate of $295 million.

While this valuation appears undemanding given the FCF growth trajectory ahead, it is in line with a B2C travel peer like Expedia (EXPE) which has mid-single digit revenue and double-digit earnings growth expectations. Moreover, I believe its discount compared to a larger B2B travel peer like Amadeus IT (OTCPK:AMADY) which trades at over 20 times FCF while growing revenue at double-digit rates, is justified.

A negative aspect with regard to GBTG's earnings is the elevated level of SBC relative to its FCF, which, as shown above, is running at close to $80 million annually. This makes the valuation multiple considerably higher on a GAAP basis and leads to meaningful dilution.

Risks

While revenue growth expectations remain muted, they could be further dampened if macroeconomic conditions worsen. Furthermore, I see digitally native competitors such as TravelPerk, which is privately held, demonstrating growth of over 50% and gaining market share.

However as I stated earlier, investor returns in GBTG hinge on execution by management with respect to realizing the synergies from the CWT acquisition. In this regard, I would point to the final report from the CMA released earlier this year, which stated:

We have found that CWT’s financial performance is weak and is likely to further weaken in the future. As a result, we have found that, while CWT remains a competitor to GBT, it is a materially weakened one.

While I have adopted a conservative approach with respect to my expectations from the CWT acquisition, given that it has been nearly 18 months since the deal was initially announced, it remains to be seen whether CWT's financial performance has held up well during the period and if GBTG's management can execute on their original plan.

GBTG Stock Remains A Hold

The valuation at 13 times FY 2026 FCF is a reasonable price to pay for this market leader, which offers low revenue growth but continued margin expansion potential. Even if revenue growth remains muted, FCF growth in the medium term should be supported by synergies from the CWT acquisition and falling interest expenses from deleveraging. However, I argue that the execution risks involved as well as the high SBC make the investment case less compelling. I am therefore maintaining my Hold rating on the stock.

data.title: First Watch Restaurant: Brunch Boom, Earnings On The Side
data.description:
First Watch Restaurant Group (FWRG), the full-service daytime chain, blew past all the bearish chatter and delivered a strong quarter—stronger than even I expected.

That said, I did nail one thing: the market wasn't focused on same-store sales. It was all about the improved full-year guidance.

As I pointed out before, First Watch was up against easier comps this quarter and took a more measured approach to pricing (currently raising prices below inflation).

Perhaps resilience in Florida combined with softer traffic comparisons could propel it to low single digits for Q2.

In fact, this stole the show, since behind all this, something seems to have been forgotten: First Watch missed EPS estimates, missing by at least $0.02, reporting it at $0.03.

Well, this isn't a big deal, is it?

It depends. A few cents might not raise eyebrows at first, but once you realize they came at the cost of a 330 bp year-over-year drop in restaurant-level margins (now down to 18.6%), it starts to look a lot less harmless.

And keep in mind: as First Watch continues buying back franchises, it becomes even more exposed to rising operating costs. That's actually the main reason adjusted EBITDA fell 13.8% year-over-year (or $4.9 million), landing at $30.4 million.

Since my last post here in mid-July, First Watch shares have risen just over 1.4% and are rising by another low single digit in pre-market trading. This outperformed the S&P by a small margin, which was up 0.7% in the same period.

Highest Same-Store Sales Since FY 2023

In Q2 FY 2025, it felt like First Watch was riding the casual dining wave more than getting dragged down by the usual daytime dining headwinds.

If you’ve been keeping up with Goulart’s Restaurant Stocks lately, you’ve probably seen the signs pointing in that direction:

  1. Data from the NRA indicates that the restaurant industry improved sequentially from March to May after a very weak February. Of course, recent NRA data indicates that June likely represented a sequential slowdown compared to May (a 0.1% drop), but that doesn't matter much in the Q2 story;
  2. Casual dining led the 'Top Performers' in literally every month of the year (except for February, when fast-food came out on top and only led for one month, but with casual dining still topping the pack). This is easy to explain, they're simply showing better value-per-dollar than fast-food and fast-casual. Whether through fixed-price promotions, early-bird dining, or loyalty program discounts;
  3. Regions like California, Arizona, and Texas were the underdogs in the quarter, even though chains like Texas Roadhouse (TXRH) and Chili's (EAT) performed well there. The Mid-Atlantic and Southeast were particularly strong regions.

First Watch is technically a casual dining FSR, with a strong footprint in the Mid-Atlantic and Southeast—even though Texas is actually its second-biggest market.

But I still saw two issues: their price points are on the higher side, and they're focused on daytime meals.

Mixed feelings about that.

I wasn’t ruling out a traffic rebound, but I wasn’t fully sold either. After all, traffic had been falling for eight quarters in a row (two full years!) and the small drop of 0.7% year-on-year in Q1 already indicated that they were almost turning positive.

At the end of the quarter, First Watch reported same-store sales growth of 3.5% year-over-year, improving 280 bp sequentially.

Just take a look at how this played out over the quarters:

Breaking this down, we have traffic growing 2% year-over-year (improving sequentially by at least 270 bp) and average check per guest growing 1.5% year-over-year (up 10 bp sequentially).

I don't have data on the average check per guest breakdown, but I imagine this is a result of low-single-digit pricing year-over-year offset by a slightly negative mix. Ultimately, the average check grew below inflation, which shows they are keeping pricing stable.

Below is a compilation of First Watch's traffic and average check growth data from recent quarters:

You can see in the chart above that both traffic is facing increasingly easier comparisons, while there is a tendency for them not to stretch prices much, at least during FY 2024 and FY 2025. With traffic turning sideways, the trend is for average check growth to remain in the low single digits while traffic carries the brunt.

Pipeline on Fire, But Pay Attention to a Few Things

Beyond the traffic rebound, what really seemed to excite the market was the growth story behind this chain—which now has 600 locations across the country.

In addition to the classic franchise acquisitions they've been making for some time (often at low multiples, as I mentioned in previous posts), they've been opening many new restaurants. This quarter alone, 17 new restaurants were opened, with another 19 franchisees acquired (in markets like North Carolina, South Carolina, and Missouri).

The goal is bold and remains on the table: 62 to 67 restaurants open for the entire FY 2025.

To gain this momentum and partially circumvent the Trump tariffs, First Watch is leveraging the websites of other restaurant chains that are closing to convert them into its own.

This is a strategy that other chains like El Pollo Loco (LOCO) are also implementing.

But when thinking about greenfield construction, consider a few things:

  • Last time I checked, building a First Watch required approximately $1.6 million—which is quite inexpensive considering it's an FSR. Legacy locations are less than 4,000 square feet, and are between 1,000 and 1,500 square feet smaller than the new locations they've been building recently. Taking this into account and adding a 10% pressure from tariffs, we can imagine the new units costing around $1.8 to $2 million;
  • They are currently generating AUVs of $2.3 million (about $700,000 above where they were in FY 2019). The long-term goal is to generate AUVs exceeding $2.7 million;
  • Restaurant-level margins have been a source of much discussion. With Food, Beverage, and Packaging costs, as well as labor, rising rapidly, First Watch reported them at 18.6% in Q2 FY 2025 (up from 21.8% in Q2 FY 2024). This gives us (assuming AUVs of $2.3 million) an annual unit-level EBITDA of $427,800.

This is interesting, as new locations are showing a return of between 21% and 23%, depending on construction costs, at this point, well below the 35% they're targeting in the long term.

If we consider the $2.7 million AUV they're aiming for, with current margins, we find a return of between 25% and 28%.

So, you can see that they clearly need to leverage these margins if they want to achieve the ROI they're aiming for.

But don't get me wrong, with what they're currently cooking (AUVs of $2.3 million and a restaurant-level margin of 18.6%), they come out ahead of many FSRs out there.

For example, if we consider the high-end construction costs ($2 million), we find a payback period of almost 5 years and an IRR of 21%.

This is light years ahead of Applebee's (7-year payback and 14.5% IRR), Bubba's 33 (TXRH) (6-year payback and 17.1% IRR), and even surpasses the lean locations of BJ's Restaurants (BJRI) (5-year payback and 19.5% IRR).

But it still lags behind names like Kura Sushi (KRUS), Cheesecake Factory (CAKE), and even STK (STKS).

So, it seems obvious to me: they need to reduce their cost centers if they want to keep greenfield expansion financially attractive.

To give you an idea, Food, Beverage, and Packaging represent 23.6% of total sales (an increase of 180 bp year-over-year).

There's pressure from raw materials like eggs (which are already dropping in price and should give the company some breathing room going forward), bacon, coffee, and avocados.

Pricing is a key piece of the puzzle here.

The only reason they're able to keep food costs under 30% is because they charge premium prices. But if commodity inflation keeps creeping up and they're forced to hold price increases in the low single digits, we could see those food costs spike a bit.

Not dramatically, though. Their premium positioning gives them enough cushion to keep it relatively in check.

In my opinion, labor costs are a more dangerous thing at the moment. They increased from 32.8% of revenue to 33.2% even with the company investing in AI workforce forecasting and a reservation system.

Wage inflation was 3.9%, above the period's pricing.

These two reasons make me skeptical about an improvement in restaurant-level margins at the moment.

But there's no denying they're growing—and fast!

Financial Recap

First Watch reported total revenue of $307.9 million for the quarter—up 19.1% year-over-year. This is the highest year-over-year growth since Q2 FY 2024, when revenue grew over 19.5% year-over-year.

Remember, not all of this came from same-store sales. In fact, the bulk came from the 149 restaurants (opened and not comparable or acquired from franchises) they've added to their portfolio since then.

As I mentioned earlier, adding more company-owned restaurants means you're taking on more operating risk. And unless you're running a tight ship at the unit level, that can really weigh on your bottom line.

That's exactly what happened here—restaurant-level margins dropped 330 basis points to 18.6%, and adjusted EBITDA came in at $30.4 million, down $4.9 million from the same quarter last year.

On the bottom line, they reported a diluted EPS of $0.03, much lower than the $0.14 they reported in the same period last year.

Management also updated guidance for all of FY 2025:

  • Positive single-digit percentage growth in same-restaurant sales, with flat to slightly positive growth in same-restaurant traffic;
  • Total revenue growth of approximately 20%, with a net impact of 400 bp from completed acquisitions. This means revenue for FY 2025 is estimated at $1.21 billion;
  • Between 62 and 67 new restaurants systemwide, including 55 and 58 new company-owned restaurants and 7 and 9 new franchised restaurants, with 3 planned company-owned restaurant closures;
  • Commodity cost inflation is projected for fiscal year 2025 to be in the 5% to 7% range, down from the previous high single-digit levels. Restaurant labor cost inflation is still expected to be in the 3% to 4% range;
  • Adjusted EBITDA to $119 million to $123 million (previous projection at $114 million to $119 million);
  • Capex between $148 million and $152 million (previously $150 million to $160 million).

Remember, they're basically telling us that both EBITDA is expected to be higher than expected, and there will be less pressure on Capex (likely to be used for acquisitions), leading them to issue more debt to cover the cash shortfall.

While improved, the EBITDA guidance of between $119 million and $123 million is still below what they had guided for at the end of FY 2024 ($124 million to $130 million), before lowering it in Q1.

Above $18: Is It Worth Buying?

In case you forgot, I've had a $16 price target on First Watch for a while now—anything in the $14s has looked like a pretty solid entry point, with an exit around $18.

But now that they’ve reviewed guidance again, it’s time to take another look at the numbers.

This time around, I'm running a different kind of comps model.

Let’s assume that three years from now, First Watch slows its unit growth, stabilizes margins, and starts delivering more predictable EBITDA gains. In that scenario, it wouldn't make sense to apply a high-growth multiple anymore. Instead, I'd peg it in the range of 12x to 14x EV/EBITDA — right in line with other mature FSR names.

In this sense, I'm considering EBITDA between $160 and $170 million for FY 2028.

This would give us exit prices between $18 and $25. Discounting this to present value at a 7.5% discount rate, we'd get a price target range between $14.50 and $20. The midpoint of this is $17.25, not far from where we were before.

Wall St. has a consensus price target of $21.50 on this stock, so even if you go with the bulls, the upside from here is just over 13%.

That's not exactly what I'd call an asymmetric bet, especially for a growth stock.

So, I'm sticking with my 'Hold' rating for now. I might nudge my price target up slightly, to somewhere in the $16.50 to $17.50 range, but that's about it. To be honest, I don’t see this stock breaking into the $20s anytime soon. I’d much rather wait for a pullback below $15 before revisiting my thesis from a valuation standpoint.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: SoundHound AI: You Might Regret Buying More Now
data.description:
SoundHound AI: Scaling Up Its Automotive Growth Engine

Wait a minute. I asked previously where the breakout was. With the incredible surge that saw the stock of SoundHound AI, Inc. (NASDAQ:SOUN) notched a gain of more than 25% to close Friday's session, I think bullish investors might have just gotten what they asked for.

The Voice AI platform company has made highly encouraging strides to expand into new growth verticals beyond its core automotive offering. SoundHound AI has accelerated the growth of its automotive business, continually expanding the array of partnerships and bolstering the assessed multi-billion dollar TAM that it has targeted to scale over time.

In my previous SOUN write-up, I made it clear that I was confident in its credibility to continue ramping up its Voice AI opportunity against bigger rivals/big tech integrators. Yet, I also questioned whether the steep premium implied in SOUN's valuation still deserves a Buy rating?

Hence, I thought it's heartening to know that its most pivotal automotive business remains the key growth and monetization driver behind the recent stellar results. Not only does it lend due weight to the market's optimism, but I believe it also helps validate SOUN's value proposition on broadening its go-to-market into new direct or indirect verticals in the next few years.

Accordingly, SoundHound AI emphasized that the automotive pillar played a monumental role in its stellar 217% YoY revenue spurt, marking a new quarterly record for SOUN. It's crucial for us to consider that the foundational vector in SoundHound's market share gains in the monolithic opportunity still requires scaling with the range of auto OEMs.

Therefore, I believe the Voice AI company has demonstrated that credibly, as it indicated further tie-ups with another "three global brands," that incorporated its in-vehicle chat assistant technology stack in the US. Also, it has pursued its global expansion plans, as SOUN has also secured a partnership with a Chinese automaker that reportedly represents "millions of units powering a prominent auto brand that's winning and expanding globally."

Hence, I believe it goes to show that having a proprietary Polaris foundation model has proffered it with substantive differentiation advantages by helping to lower the costs of adoption while also improving the speed and accuracy (35% accuracy edge over peers and 4x reduction in latency). That should help position the third and potentially massive pillar in Voice commerce constructively to transform into one of SOUN's growth opportunities, leveling up its ARPU economics while also paving the way to accelerated profitability metrics over time.

SoundHound AI: Peak Growth Worries Still Dominate

Nevertheless, the company also noted that such deal momentum may not be replicated every quarter, reminding investors of the need to temper overoptimism. Also, while Wall Street has raised SOUN's near-term outlook in view of the company's upgraded guidance, analysts are still reticent in projecting similar growth momentum through 2026.

Although the path toward adjusted EBITDA profitability exiting 2025 remains on track, the company might have to demonstrate that it could cross-sell its monetization levers more quickly, with the market plausibly scrutinizing future updates from management on its commerce prospects.

Hence, I think it's opportune for us to be judicious about SoundHound's opportunity set while giving the company more time to justify its high-growth premium.

Buy Or Sell SOUN Stock?

In all honesty, SOUN appears to have approached and also retested the breakout zone at just under the $15 level. However, we must also appreciate the high volatility inherent in its price action, so it's quite challenging to ascertain an exact level to assess whether the breakout could prove decisive.

I must highlight that the stock's uptrend bias indicates that dip-buying cadence over the past four months has been incredibly resilient. And that goes to show that selling fervor is seemingly lacking, as if the bears have gone into hibernation (but could still reappear later with vengeance).

With SOUN's forward EV/revenue metric of almost 28x (way above tech median of 3x), do bulls really believe that they have a path to retake the stock's December 2024 peak?

Instead, from my vantage point, I assess that SOUN's buying momentum could still face significant hurdles if the market doesn't expect its Q2 revenue surge to be replicated in the second half. And, I think unless management can guide to a much improved profitability path, the right thing to do for now is to continue watching the developments from the sidelines.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

data.title: Boston Scientific Corporation Is Expensive But Worth It
data.description:
Overview

The other day, a colleague referred to an investment opportunity as a “forever company.” He intended to implant the notion that there exist certain stocks an investor should never sell in toto; i.e., it is okay to take some profits along the way and diversify. Despite an F Factor Grade for valuation from Seeking Alpha, we believe Boston Scientific Corporation (BSX) deserves our long-term buy rating for management’s foresight, strong balance sheet, highly profitable operations, commitment to R&D, and the company’s leading position in the development of AI-energized medical devices. The stock’s proven track record for growing one’s portfolio is admirable.

A forever stock like BSX boasts long-lasting quality, fostering growth and profitability. Its indefinite duration is due to management’s commercial foresight, investment in R&D, consistent development and marketing of new products, and building worldwide enduring partnerships. The company develops medical devices, procedures, and therapies. Leading products by medical specialties include

  • Critical Care products
  • Electrophysiology products
  • Female Pelvic Medicine products
  • Gastroenterology products
  • Gastrointestinal Surgery products
  • Gynecology products
  • Interventional Cardiology products
  • Interventional Radiology products
  • Neurological Surgery products
  • Otolaryngology products
  • Pain Management products
  • Pulmonology products
  • Urology products
  • Vascular Surgery products

Tailwinds to Higher Share Price

BCC Research claims the global medical device market is growing at a CAGR rate of 9.8% and will reach $1.3T by 2029. It will grow faster as AI advances, is used in medical devices, and will revolutionize diagnostics and treatments. Add the fact that the world’s population is aging, increasing the demand for more medical devices and treatment solutions. The U.S. Census Bureau reported on June 25, 2025, that “the U.S. population age 65 and older rose by 3.1% (to 61.2 million)” between 2023 and 2024. Boston Scientific’s integrated strategies, according to Klover.AI, put the company in a leadership position to monetize its strategies.

Klover.ai, an AI researcher, concludes

Boston Scientific (BSX) is poised to dominate the AI-enabled medical technology landscape through a deliberate, multi-pronged strategy that integrates advanced artificial intelligence directly into its core, high-growth product portfolios, particularly in cardiology. This product-led AI approach, fueled by robust financial performance, strategic acquisitions, and the cultivation of unique data and talent moats, creates a more defensible and synergistic competitive advantage than the platform-centric or less-focused strategies of its peers. The company’s strategy is built on four key pillars. First, a clear C-suite vision and exceptional financial results provide the mandate and capital for sustained, long-term AI investment. Second, BSX is developing and acquiring AI tools like HeartLogic, BeatLogic, and AVVIGO+ that are not standalone software but are deeply embedded in revenue-generating devices, enhancing their clinical value and creating a sticky ecosystem for healthcare providers. Third, a disciplined M&A and venture capital strategy systematically acquires proven AI technologies and nurtures early-stage innovation to fill pipeline gaps and accelerate market entry. Finally, BSX is cultivating two critical, hard-to-replicate assets: a world-class AI and machine learning talent pool and vast, proprietary, longitudinal datasets from millions of implanted devices, which are essential for training next-generation algorithms.

Valuation

On July 23, ’25, the company reported “another excellent quarter—marked by exceptional top-line performance—that delivered margin expansion and prioritized investment for future growth.” However, almost all the valuation metrics used by Seeking Alpha rate an F or D+ factor grade in comparison to the Health Care sector's medians. Meanwhile, more than 30 analysts assess the stock as a buy opportunity, though the shares are selling at $104 each, near their 52-week high of $108.94. The consensus is that the stock will outperform going forward.

A fair value price per share is $105 by multiplying the 35 PE ratio times EPS of $3. If the company beats the estimated EPS, the average target price can top $120 in the next two quarters, adding another 10% to 15% for momentum. Its 9-month price performance is up 22.5% compared to the sector median of -17.4% and up 43.68% for one year’s performance compared to -13% for Health Care companies’ stocks. Fintel puts the short interest float at a low 1.32% with 4.45 days to cover.

Q2 ’25 highlights from the financials are the 22.8% increase in revenue over Q2 ‘24; GAAP net income rose to $0.53 per share from the estimated $0.47 per share; and sales increased in each; the U.S. by 30.7%, in EMEA by nearly 7%, Asia-Pacific by about 16%, Latin America and Canada by 6% on average, and most importantly in Emerging Markets by about 12%. Cash from operations spiked each of the last 4 years and tops $4.285B TTM. Free cash flow per share is $2.37 TTM, up from $1.81 in Dec ’24.

Risks Warranting Buys on Dips

Some of the challenges facing Boston Scientific include investors’ demand for the company to consistently build the ROI through the implementation of its AI plan. The company’s growth in revenue is partly attributable to its aggressive M&A moves. There have been, on average, 2 acquisitions per year for the last 5 years, all doing business in the theater of operations as the core business of Boston Scientific; that is another indication of how management has been sticking to the knitting.

Insiders and hedge funds sold more shares than they purchased in the last quarter as the stock approached $100 per share. Funds sold 892,000 shares. Insiders sold $1.2M worth of shares in the last 3 months. However, more funds owned shares almost every quarter. In Q2 ’23, 76 funds reportedly owned BSX; 90 in Q2 ’24; and 109 at the end of Q1 ’25.

Headwinds the company can face include changes in regulations for medical devices from the USHHS, cybersecurity pressures, and production costs versus payment affordability. It is important to watch for impacts on income that Boston Scientific generates from Medicaid and Medicare, with cutbacks in federal programs expected to impact revenues by the end of 2025.

Takeaway

At the current valuation, with insiders selling shares, and threats to revenue from HHS cutbacks, retail value investors should act prudently and watch for buy opportunities on dips in the share price. Nevertheless, Boston Scientific appears to have a bright future in a growing industry, especially selling into Asia-Pacific and Emerging Markets. The next two quarterly reports are important to watch to see if the company can maintain its high gross profit margin (~68%) and revenue growth. Like Mark Twain's perspective, long-term growth is best achieved by embracing continuous improvement over perfection, accepting change as a part of growth, and recognizing that regret often stems from inaction rather than action.

data.title: Abbott Laboratories Q2 Earnings: Dividends Don't Lie
data.description:
ABT Stock: Previous Thesis And New Developments

I last analyzed Abbott Laboratories (ABT) in June 2024 with an article titled “Abbott Laboratories: Why I Agree With Wall Street On This One”. That article was triggered by the change in the prevailing sentiment around the stock at that time. I rated it as a buy based on these following considerations:

Wall Street analysts have a 'strong buy' rating on ABT stock, and we agree with such bullish sentiment. Despite some risks, ABT stock has the potential to outperform the overall market by a large margin. The key return drivers include its strong growth potential, high ROCE, and a discounted valuation.

Since then, a few material changes have emerged and motivated this follow-up coverage. First, the stock price has appreciated (and outpaced the broader market as seen in the chart below) substantially, calling for a re-examination of its valuation risks. Second, a few recent geopolitical events, most notably the tariff rate changes and the new Executive Order that President Trump signed to lower drug prices, have added considerable uncertainties to the healthcare sector as a whole. Finally, the company has also reported its latest earnings and operations, with a few notable updates. The goal of this follow-up article is to consider these changes and re-examine its reward/risk ratio. In the end, I keep seeing an attractive investment opportunity and reiterate my buy rating.

ABT Stock: FQ2 Earnings Report Recap

Let me start with the FQ2 earnings report [ER] that ABT recently released on July 17 (see the chart below). As seen, Abbott reported showed a normalized EPS of $1.26, which was in line with expectations for the quarter. Revenue was reported at $11.14B, slightly exceeding projections by $84.11M. Looking ahead to the upcoming quarter, the company's EPS is projected to grow mildly by about 3% sequentially to a normalized EPS of $1.30 and a GAAP EPS of $1.04. The latest revisions were dominated by downward revisions (19 vs. 3 upward revisions only).

I indeed see good reasons for the above sentiment judging from the updates provided in ER and also the macroscopic uncertainties ongoing (i.e., the potential of tariff changes and the drug price control). Looking closer at its FQ2 growth drivers, I consider the momentum in its Medical Device business to be leading one. Sales on this front increased 13% in the quarter on the back of double-digit gains in diabetes care, heart failure, and structural heart. All of these could be subject to drug price scrutiny and also tariff rates. Furthermore, FQ2 results also showed weakness in its Diagnostics segment (-1%). This largely reflects continued pressure on COVID-19 testing-related sales and volume-based procurement programs in China, which is another area that could be sensitive to tariff and trade disputes.

ABT Stock: Latest Payout Declaration

Past experiences have taught me that the more uncertainties there are, the more I must focus on return vectors with the least amount of ambiguity. As a long-established dividend aristocrat, I could not think of a vector that is more reliable than ABT’s dividend payouts.

In tandem with its FQ2 ER, Abbott also declared its third dividend payout for 2025, as you can see from the following table. The company has declared a regular quarterly dividend of $0.5900 per share with an ex-dividend date of July 15, 2025. This translates into an annual dividend growth rate of 7.2%, a quite healthy growth rate in my view.

ABT Stock: Valuation

In terms of valuation, the P/E ratios now exhibit a range of grades due to the sizable price appreciation since my last writing. For example, the Non-GAAP P/E [TTM] is now 26.95, which is a substantial 66% higher than the sector median of 16.18 and also more than 7% higher than its 5-year average of 25.17. Similarly, the FWD P/E (on a non-GAAP basis again) is also substantially (by more than 50%) higher than the sector median of 25.68 and also about 4% above its own historical average.

However, if you subscribe to my earlier notion that ABT’s dividends represent a much more reliable earning metric than accounting EPS, dividend yield then is a more reliable metric for assessing ABT's valuation risks than P/E ratios. P/E ratios, especially those based on non-GAAP, are susceptible to being skewed by accounting adjustments, one-time gains or losses, and a company's specific reporting choices.

While dividend payouts have none of these ambiguities. Turning to the dividend yield chart shown below, I see the stock at a fair valuation. To wit, the current dividend yield is 1.76%, which is only slightly below the 10-year average of 1.81%. Also note that its yield has fluctuated historically between a quite tractable range, spending most of its time between 2.5% and 1.2%, as seen.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

Before closing, let me revisit the issues of macroscopic uncertainties. My view is that the impact of the Trump administration’s plan to lower drugs is difficult to quantify at this stage with the amount of specifics available. But the impacts from a few other macroeconomic headwinds seem to be substantial based on the information provided in its Q2 ER. For example, my estimate points to ~$700 million top-line headwind due to the fading COVID-19 test demand and pricing pressure from China. The country buys medical products from ABT in bulk at significant discounts. Based on the information the company provided in the Q2 ER, I also expect ~$200 million in tariff-related costs for FY 2025 based on tariff rates at that time. Since then, the Trump administration has proposed to place 250% tariffs on pharmaceuticals if the industry could propose a satisfactory plan to lower drug prices.

To close, the goal of this ABT follow-up is to re-examine the reward/risk profile given the new catalysts surrounding the stock since my last writing. These main catalysts considered are the valuation expansion since my last writing, the ongoing tariff and trade disputes, and finally the potential of drug price changes. I certainly see some near-term challenges. However, I do not see any of these issues fundamentally altering Abbott’s long-term moat – best reflected by its healthy dividend growth in my model. The underlying moat across its core business segments remains intact, in my view. I also expect the moat to be further augmented by a cycle of new product launches from its pipeline in the coming years. Finally, dividend yield suggests the stock is trading near a fair valuation too. With these considerations, I will conclude with a reiteration of my buy rating on the stock.
